subject: Astronomy & Space
title: Supernova Remnant Nebulae
description: A quick, practical guide to Supernova Remnant Nebulae.

hook.text: When a star explodes, its debris expands into a beautiful nebula. These remnants glow for thousands of years.

buildup.text: The expanding shell shocks surrounding gas, heating it to millions of degrees. It glows in X-rays, radio, and visible light.

discovery.text: The Veil Nebula is a remnant from a supernova about 10,000 years ago. Its delicate filaments stretch across the sky.

twist.text: Over time, the remnant slows down and merges with the surrounding gas, enriching it with heavy elements for future stars.

climax.text: Our own solar system formed from gas enriched by ancient supernova remnants. We literally live in recycled stardust.

punchline.text: Shells of debris. The stardust that built our world came from remnants like these.

quiz.question: How do supernova remnants contribute to the formation of new solar systems?
options: They enrich surrounding gas with heavy elements that become part of new stars and planets
They compress nearby gas so rapidly that planets form instantly
They emit radiation that triggers chemical reactions creating organic molecules
